title: AdamW: Decoupled Weight Decay
concept: AdamW refines Adam by separating (decoupling) weight decay — a regularization technique — from the main adaptive gradient update, leading to better generalization. It's now a very common default in modern large-model training.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what two things does Adam combine into one optimizer?

options.text: Momentum's velocity smoothing and RMSprop's per-parameter adaptive rates
AdaGrad's accumulation and a bigger batch size

exercises.explain: Right — Adam blends Momentum's velocity with RMSprop's adaptive rates. But there's one more common training ingredient we haven't looked at yet: weight decay. How does it interact with Adam?

cards.title: A regularization trick that got tangled up
cards.body: Weight decay is a common regularization technique: on every update, it nudges every weight a little bit toward zero, so the network can't lean too heavily on any single weight and overfit the training data. In plain SGD, this works cleanly. But when weight decay gets mixed into Adam's adaptive per-parameter math, something unexpected happens.

cards.title: AdamW: decoupled weight decay
cards.body: AdamW separates the weight decay step from Adam's main adaptive gradient update, applying it directly to the weights instead of folding it into the adaptive-rate math. "Decoupled" simply means learning from gradients and shrinking weights slightly now happen as two SEPARATE, independent steps — not one tangled-together step.

cards.title: Two separate bills, not one confusing combined charge
cards.body: Imagine paying rent and a gym membership as one single combined charge on your account — it becomes unclear how much each one actually cost you, and a change in one silently changes the other. In plain Adam, weight decay and the adaptive gradient update were bundled together like that combined charge. AdamW sends two SEPARATE, clearly itemized charges instead.

cards.title: The standard optimizer behind modern language models
cards.body: Training a large language model — the kind of transformer model behind modern chatbots — almost always uses AdamW rather than plain Adam, precisely because its decoupled weight decay noticeably improves how well the model generalizes to new, unseen text.

cards.title: AdamW isn't a totally different optimizer
cards.body: It's easy to assume AdamW is a completely new, redesigned algorithm. It isn't — AdamW keeps ALL of Adam's momentum and adaptive-rate machinery exactly the same. The ONLY change is where and how weight decay gets applied. It's a small, surgical fix, not a redesign.

left.text:
Weight decay folded INTO the adaptive gradient update
Gets tangled with per-parameter scaling

right.text:
Weight decay applied as a SEPARATE step
Cleaner regularization, better generalization

exercises.prompt: What does AdamW change compared to plain Adam?

options.text: It applies weight decay as a separate, decoupled step instead of folding it into the adaptive gradient update
It uses a much bigger learning rate by default

exercises.explain: Correct! AdamW's core change is decoupling weight decay from the adaptive update — everything else about Adam stays the same.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Weight decay is a regularization technique that gently shrinks weights toward zero on every update, to help prevent overfitting.
exercises.explain: True! Weight decay nudges every weight slightly smaller each step so the network doesn't lean too heavily on any one weight.

exercises.sentence: AdamW ___ weight decay from Adam's main adaptive gradient update, applying it as a separate step.
exercises.explain: "Decouples" means separating weight decay into its own clean step, instead of mixing it into the adaptive-rate math.

exercises.prompt: You're training a large transformer model and want strong regularization that generalizes well to new data, without weight decay getting tangled up in Adam's per-parameter adaptive rates. Which optimizer is the standard choice?
exercises.explain: AdamW's decoupled weight decay is exactly why it's the standard for training large modern models.

exercises.prompt: A researcher notices that plain Adam's built-in weight decay interacts unpredictably with its adaptive per-parameter learning rates, sometimes over- or under-shrinking certain weights. Which optimizer directly fixes this by separating weight decay from the adaptive gradient update, and why has it become a standard default for training large modern models?

options.text: AdamW, because it decouples weight decay into its own clean step instead of tangling it with adaptive scaling, which measurably improves generalization
RMSprop, because it has no weight decay at all
Plain SGD, because it uses one shared learning rate
AdaGrad, because it accumulates every past gradient

exercises.explain: Exactly right! You've now met the full optimizer family: SGD, Momentum, Nesterov, AdaGrad, RMSprop, Adam, and AdamW. Next up: the final lesson — a decision matrix for choosing the right one for any given job.
